notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here are the big 2026 tax-planning levers many self‑employed consultants look at each year. Think of this as a high-level checklist to review with a tax pro.

1. Retirement accounts (Solo 401(k), SEP IRA, etc.)

Why this matters: These can shelter a big chunk of your consulting income from current tax and build retirement savings.

Solo 401(k) vs SEP IRA (conceptually for 2026):

Solo 401(k):

Fits: Self‑employed with variable income who want max flexibility
Contributions: Employee deferral + employer profit share
Potential max: Often higher at lower income levels because of the employee deferral
Roth option: Often available

SEP IRA:

Fits: Simpler setup, often for higher, more stable profits
Contributions: Employer profit share only
Potential max: Often similar max at high income, but less flexible
Roth option: Generally pre‑tax only

Key concepts:

Employee deferral: You can defer up to the annual 401(k) limit from your own compensation.
Employer contribution: A percentage of net self‑employment income (with IRS formulas and caps).
Traditional vs Roth:
Traditional: reduces current taxable income
Roth: no current deduction, but tax‑free withdrawals if rules are met
Deadlines:
Solo 401(k): usually must be set up by year‑end for employee deferrals; employer contributions can often be made by the tax filing deadline.
SEP IRA: can usually be opened and funded by the tax filing deadline (including extensions).

Good topics for your tax pro:

Which plan lets you make the largest contribution at your expected profit level.
Whether a Roth Solo 401(k) fits your long‑term tax picture.
2. Quarterly estimated tax payments

Why this matters: Self‑employed income usually doesn’t have withholding, so the IRS expects quarterly estimates.

Key ideas:

You’re generally covering income tax + self‑employment tax (Social Security and Medicare) through estimates.
IRS “safe harbor” rules often help you avoid penalties if you pay:
A percentage of last year’s tax, or
A percentage of this year’s expected tax (varies by income level and state).

Practical moves:

Use a separate tax savings account and move a fixed percentage of each payment there. Many people start with 25–35% of net profit and adjust.
Put the four federal due dates on your calendar (typically April, June, September, January).
3. Business deductions (ordinary and necessary expenses)

Core rule: Expenses need to be ordinary and necessary for your consulting work.

Common buckets:

Professional services: legal, accounting, tax prep, software subscriptions.
Marketing: website, ads, design, conferences, networking.
Travel and meals: business travel, client meetings (meals usually only partially deductible).
Education: courses, certifications, books directly tied to your consulting.
Equipment: computers, phones, office furniture (may be depreciated or expensed under Section 179/bonus rules).

Key concepts:

Capital vs current expenses: Some things are written off over time (depreciation) instead of all at once.
Mixed-use items: Phone, internet, car often need a business‑use percentage.
4. Home office deduction

When it applies: You use part of your home regularly and exclusively as your main place of business.

Two main methods:

Simplified method:

Flat rate per square foot up to a cap.
Easy to calculate and document.

Actual expense method:

Deduct a percentage of mortgage interest/rent, utilities, insurance, repairs, etc. based on business‑use square footage.
More recordkeeping, but potentially a bigger deduction.

Important details:

“Exclusive use” means that area isn’t used for personal stuff.
Keep a simple diagram and photos, plus utility and mortgage/rent records.
5. Health insurance and HSA

Self‑employed health insurance deduction:

Many self‑employed people can deduct health, dental, and sometimes long‑term care premiums for themselves, spouse, and dependents, within certain rules and income limits.
This is often an above‑the‑line deduction, which reduces adjusted gross income.

Health Savings Account (HSA):

You need a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP) that meets IRS rules.
Contributions:
Are pre‑tax (or deductible),
Grow tax‑deferred,
Come out tax‑free for qualified medical expenses.
A lot of people treat HSAs as a “stealth retirement account” by paying current medical costs out of pocket and letting the HSA grow.
6. Entity choice: Sole prop vs LLC vs S‑corp (conceptual)

Sole proprietor:

Default for many consultants.
Easiest to set up and maintain.
All net profit is generally subject to self‑employment tax plus income tax.

LLC (legal structure):

Often used for liability protection and flexibility.
For federal tax, a single‑member LLC is usually still taxed as a sole prop unless you elect something else.

S‑corporation election (tax treatment):

The business pays you a reasonable salary (subject to payroll taxes).
Remaining profit may be distributions, often not subject to self‑employment tax.
Can cut overall employment taxes when profits are high enough to justify:
Payroll setup,
Extra tax filings,
Reasonable compensation analysis.

Good questions for a tax pro:

At what profit level S‑corp treatment might start to be tax‑efficient.
State‑level issues (some states add extra entity taxes or fees).
7. Recordkeeping and systems

Why this matters: Clean records back up your deductions and make tax season much less painful.

Core habits:

Use separate business bank and credit card accounts.
Keep a simple bookkeeping system (spreadsheet or accounting software).
Save:
Receipts (digital is usually fine),
Invoices,
Mileage logs,
Home office calculations,
Health insurance and HSA statements.

Retention:

Many practitioners suggest keeping tax records at least 3–7 years, depending on the item and local rules.
8. Year‑end planning themes

Each fall, many self‑employed consultants:

Estimate year‑to‑date profit and project full‑year income.
Decide whether to:
Accelerate or defer income (like timing invoices),
Accelerate or defer expenses (like equipment purchases),
Increase retirement contributions before year‑end.
Review:
Whether estimated payments are on track,
Whether their entity structure still fits their income level and risk.

If you’d like, Monarch can turn this into a short, prioritized checklist (Q1 ta

Feature	Solo 401(k)	SEP IRA
Who it fits	Self‑employed with variable income, want max flexibility	Simpler setup, often for higher stable profits
Contributions	Employee deferral + employer profit share	Employer profit share only
Potential max (2026, conceptually)	Higher at lower income levels because of employee deferral	Often similar max at high income, but less flexible
Roth option	Often available	Generally pre‑tax only
